_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 9-34R Midazolam (MDHHS Reviewed 2025). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Medication Protocol Section 9-34R: Midazolam — MDHHS Reviewed 2025
indications: Seizure — active seizure (status epilepticus or prolonged seizure)
Hyperactive delirium (behavioral emergency requiring sedation for patient/provider safety)
contraindications: Shock or hemodynamic instability (relative — contact online MC; titrate carefully)
Known hypersensitivity to midazolam or benzodiazepines
precautions: Monitor respiratory rate, SpO2, and mental status continuously
Have BVM and airway management immediately available
Combining with opioids significantly increases respiratory depression risk — reduce doses
IM route for seizure: give without IV when IV not immediately available — preferred approach for active seizure
Elderly or debilitated patients: use lower end of dosing range and titrate carefully
side_effects: Paradoxical agitation (rare — more common in pediatric and elderly)
notes: Seizure adult: 10 mg IM (if no IV) OR 5 mg IV/IO; if seizure persists: repeat 5 mg IV/IO/IM/IN
Hyperactive delirium adult: 10 mg IM or IN
Chemical restraint adult: 0.1 mg/kg IM or IV; maximum 10 mg
Procedural sedation: 1-5 mg IV; maximum 0.1 mg/kg
Pediatric seizure: 0.1 mg/kg IM; maximum 10 mg

dosing.notes: Preferred when IV not immediately available; if seizure persists after 5 min: repeat 5 mg IV/IO/IM/IN

dosing.dose: 5 mg IV/IO; may repeat 5 mg if seizure persists
dosing.notes: IV route when access available; repeat once if seizure continues

dosing.dose: 10 mg IM or 10 mg IN via MAD
dosing.notes: For severe behavioral emergency; monitor airway after administration
Titrate slowly; combine with fentanyl for painful procedures when appropriate
Preferred route when IV not available; for pediatric procedural sedation see applicable protocol
